project.name: yugen
project.description: The Yugen Discord bot stack — 5 collaborative Discord bots: Koto (wordle), Kusari (word-chain), Kazu (counting), Hoshi (starboard), Iro (color codes)

nodes.summary: Adds a game type enum column and type field to the Game table, enabling differentiation between counting game variants.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Renames the Guess table to History and drops/recreates related indexes to reflect its actual purpose as a game history log.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Adds a points column to the Player table to track cumulative scoring for guild members.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Adds a word count column to track the number of words counted per game.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Removes the previously added word count column from the Game table (reverted design decision).

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Sets the default value for the Settings.cooldown column to 0, meaning no cooldown by default.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Adds saves and highscore tracking columns to the Game and Settings tables to support game save/restore and highscore features.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Corrects a previous migration that added saves/highscore columns to the wrong model by dropping and re-adding them on the correct tables.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Sets the default value for the max guild saves limit to 2.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Renames the guildSaves column to saves to align with updated Prisma schema naming conventions.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Alters the saves column type from integer to float to support fractional save counts.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Adds additional statistical tracking columns to the Player table for richer game analytics.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Renames the game word column to lastNumber to better reflect that the stored value is the most recently counted integer.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Adds shameRoleId and removeShameRoleAfterHighscore columns to the Settings table to support the shame role feature.

nodes.name: migration.sql
nodes.summary: Changes the default cooldown value in the Settings table from 0 to 9 seconds.

nodes.name: migration.sql (add_is_highscored_to_game)
nodes.summary: Adds the isHighscored boolean column to the Game table in Kazu's counting-bot database to track whether a game resulted in a new highscore.

nodes.name: migration.sql (rename_player_to_player_stats)
nodes.summary: Renames the Player table to PlayerStats in Kazu's database, updating all associated indexes and constraints to reflect the new name.

nodes.name: migration.sql (move_saves_to_its_own_table)
nodes.summary: Creates the PlayerSaves table to store per-user saves data (saves count, max saves, last vote time) separately from PlayerStats, and drops the saves-related columns from the old location.

nodes.name: migration.sql (add_bot_update_channel_id_and_clear_shame)
nodes.summary: Adds the botUpdatesChannelId and removeShameRoleAfterHighscore columns to the Settings table to support bot update announcements and conditional shame-role removal on new records.

nodes.name: migration.sql (add_history_message_id)
nodes.summary: Adds optional messageId and lastMessageId columns to the History and Game tables respectively, enabling the bot to track and update Discord message references.

nodes.name: migration.sql (add_max_saves_for_players)
nodes.summary: Adds the maxSaves column to the PlayerSaves table with a default of 2, allowing per-user save-slot limits distinct from server-level settings.

nodes.name: migration.sql (add_indexes)
nodes.summary: Adds database indexes on Game.guildId, History.(userId,gameId), PlayerSaves.userId, PlayerStats.(userId,guildId), and Settings.guildId to improve query performance across the Kazu bot schema.

nodes.name: migration.sql (move_cooldown_to_seconds)
nodes.summary: Migrates the Settings.cooldown column value from minutes to seconds by multiplying existing values by 60, converting the stored unit for the counting-game cooldown.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: The README introduces the Yugen monorepo: five independent Discord bots (Hoshi, Iro, Kazu, Kusari, Koto) that all live in the same repository and share a common foundation. Reading this first establishes the mental model — each bot is a standalone Go binary with its own purpose, but they are developed, deployed, and structured in a uniform way.

tour.title: Monorepo Workspace
tour.description: The go.work file at the repo root declares a Go workspace that unifies six Go modules — one per bot plus one shared module — without requiring them to be published. This is the structural glue of the monorepo: it lets each bot import shared/ directly as a local module during development, and each bot's go.mod pins only its own direct dependencies.
tour.languageLesson: Go workspaces (go.work) allow multiple modules in one repository to reference each other by local path without needing version tags or replace directives. The 'use' directive adds each module directory to the workspace; the go.work.sum file tracks checksums for workspace dependencies.

tour.title: Shared Configuration
tour.description: shared/config/config.go defines the single Config struct that every bot reads from environment variables at startup. It covers Discord credentials, database URL, API server address, Loki logging, Sentry, Top.GG vote integration, and per-bot extras. Because all bots share this struct, adding a new env-driven flag is a one-place change — the struct's env tags are parsed by caarlos0/env at boot.
tour.languageLesson: Go struct tags like `env:"DISCORD_TOKEN,required"` are read at runtime via reflection by libraries such as caarlos0/env. The 'required' option causes Load() to return an error if the variable is absent, enforcing fail-fast configuration validation before the bot tries to connect.

tour.title: Bot Entry Point
tour.description: apps/hoshi/cmd/bot/main.go is the canonical entry point for any bot in this stack — every bot's main.go follows the same pattern. It loads .env with godotenv, creates a named zap logger, builds the DI container, initialises the Discord session, starts the cron scheduler, then runs the Fiber HTTP server inside an errgroup goroutine. Shutdown is coordinated by signal.NotifyContext so all subsystems drain cleanly when the process receives SIGTERM.
tour.languageLesson: errgroup.WithContext wires a shared cancellation context to a group of goroutines: if any returns an error the context is cancelled and the rest should stop. Pairing it with signal.NotifyContext means a Ctrl-C or SIGTERM cancels the context that both the signal handler and the HTTP server are watching, so the shutdown sequence is deterministic.

tour.title: Dependency Injection Container
tour.description: Each bot's inits/di.go builds a sarulabs/di container that wires every dependency — bot session, config, Prisma client, services, and bot-specific constants — before any Discord handler or HTTP route is registered. The shared half of this setup is delegated to shared/inits/di.go (InitSharedDI), which registers the config, Discord bot session, and cron scheduler that every bot needs. Bot-specific services are then appended on top.
tour.languageLesson: sarulabs/di implements a service-locator style DI container. Each di.Def has a Build function (constructor) and an optional Close function (destructor). Calling container.DeleteWithSubContainers() in a deferred statement at startup ensures every Close is called in reverse registration order during shutdown — the database connection disconnects before the bot session closes.

tour.title: Discord Bot Initialisation
tour.description: apps/hoshi/internal/inits/discord.go shows how a bot wires itself to Discord after the DI container is ready. It sets the required gateway intents, registers shared listeners (logging, Prometheus metrics, vote webhooks), registers bot-specific listeners (guild joins, reaction events), calls InitCommands to load slash commands into discordgoplus's router, then opens the WebSocket connection. Every other bot follows the identical pattern.
tour.languageLesson: discordgo gateway intents are a bitmask that tells Discord which events to send over the WebSocket. Requesting only what you need (e.g., IntentsGuildMessageReactions for Hoshi) reduces traffic and is required for certain privileged events like MessageContent.

tour.title: Slash Command Modules
tour.description: shared/utils/register-commands-module.go defines the CommandsModule interface and RegisterCommandModules helper used by every bot. A module is simply a struct with a Commands() method that returns a slice of discordgoplus.Command. The helper loops over all modules, registers each command with the router, and logs what was registered. Shared commands (help, vote, donate, invite, support) live in shared/slashcommands/ and are reused across every bot.
tour.languageLesson: Go interfaces are satisfied implicitly — any struct with a Commands() method satisfies CommandsModule without a declaration. The optional CommandsAndMessageComponentsModule and ModalsModule interfaces extend this via type assertion (if a, ok := m.(ModalsModule); ok), so a module only needs to implement what it provides.

tour.title: Event Listeners and Debouncing
tour.description: apps/hoshi/internal/listeners/reaction.go is a representative event listener. It registers two Discord events — MessageReactionAdd and MessageReactionRemove — and routes both through a reactionDebouncer that uses sync.Map to coalesce rapid reaction changes on the same message into a single 500 ms delayed call to StarboardService.CheckReaction. This prevents the starboard from flickering when a user quickly adds and removes a star.
tour.languageLesson: sync.Map is Go's concurrent-safe map, designed for cases where keys are written once and read many times, or where different goroutines work on disjoint key sets. LoadAndDelete atomically retrieves and removes an entry, which is exactly what the debouncer needs to stop an in-flight timer before scheduling a new one.

tour.title: Business Logic: Services
tour.description: apps/hoshi/internal/services/starboard.go is the largest service in the stack and illustrates the service pattern used throughout. The StarboardService holds its dependencies (Prisma client, SettingsService, Discord bot, config) as private struct fields injected via the DI container. CheckReaction orchestrates the full starboard flow: validate settings, check ignored channels, resolve thread parent channels, look up matching starboard configurations, count valid reactions, then create/update/delete the starboard post and its database Log record.

tour.title: Database Schema with Prisma
tour.description: apps/hoshi/prisma/schema.prisma defines the three tables that back the starboard feature: Settings (per-guild configuration including threshold and ignored channels), Starboards (each configured starboard mapping an emoji+source channel to a target channel), and Log (the append-only record of which original messages have been posted, used to prevent duplicates and to update or delete posts when reaction counts change). The generated Prisma client in prisma/db/ is committed alongside the schema.
tour.languageLesson: prisma-client-go generates a type-safe Go client from the Prisma schema. The //go:generate directive at the top of main.go re-runs the generator. The binaryTargets field lists which Prisma query engine binaries to embed, which is why the db/ directory contains large _gen.go files with the engine binary baked in as a byte slice.

tour.title: Iro: The DB-Free Exception
tour.description: apps/iro/internal/listeners/color.go shows how Iro deliberately breaks the bot template. It has no database, no slash commands, and no services — just a single listener that scans every incoming message for hex color codes using a regex. When a match is found it generates a color swatch PNG, uploads it as a temporary application emoji, reacts to the message with it, caches the color in a timedmap, and then deletes the emoji after 5 seconds. A subsequent reaction by a user on that emoji triggers the detailed embed reply.

tour.title: Observability: Metrics and HTTP API
tour.description: shared/listeners/metrics.go registers Discord event handlers that keep Prometheus gauges up to date: guild count, channel count, registered interactions, and heartbeat latency. These are scraped by the Fiber HTTP server started in shared/inits/api.go, which exposes /api/metrics (Prometheus), /api/monitor (Fiber dashboard), and a /api/vote webhook endpoint. Every bot runs this same HTTP sidecar, so all five bots can be scraped by the same Prometheus instance.
tour.languageLesson: promauto.NewGauge registers a Prometheus gauge at package initialisation time — no explicit registration call needed. The gauge is a package-level var that any file in the metrics package can update. promhttp.Handler() serves the default registry, which collects all promauto-registered metrics automatically.

tour.title: Containerisation and CI/CD
tour.description: Dockerfile.hoshi is the production build pipeline in a single file. The builder stage copies only the go.mod/go.sum files first (to exploit Docker layer caching for dependencies), then copies the full source and runs `make build-hoshi`. The final stage is FROM scratch — the absolute minimum base — with only the compiled binary and the system CA certificates. Each bot has its own Dockerfile following the same multi-stage pattern. The matching GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/hoshi.yml) triggers on pushes that touch hoshi or shared code and pushes the resulting image to GHCR.
tour.languageLesson: FROM scratch produces an image with no OS, no shell, and no package manager — only what you explicitly COPY in. Go binaries compiled with CGO_ENABLED=0 are self-contained and work in scratch images. The CA certificates COPY is required for TLS connections (Discord's HTTPS API) because scratch has no certificate store.
